title: Catch-All Domain Mass Account Creation with Card Testing Spray
narrative: Fraudsters exploit catch-all email domains (protonmail.com, mail.ru, outlook.com) to create 20-50+ accounts with randomized prefixes in under 6 hours, enabling parallel card testing without reputation overlap. Each account tests 1-3 stolen cards with $1-$15 transactions (ProductCD='W' or 'C'). The domain accepts any prefix, so fraudster generates emails like x7k2p@protonmail.com, m9wd@mail.ru with zero validation. Successful cards graduate to $200-$800 purchases within 24 hours from the same billing address (addr1) but different email prefixes. Detection requires cross-prefix aggregation: monitoring P_emaildomain transaction velocity rather than full email. Typical signature: 15+ unique email prefixes on same domain, same IP or device, card1_txn_count_1day spikes 30-80x baseline, 60-90% decline rate on test transactions, AVS mismatch on 40%+ attempts, then sudden authorization on larger amounts.
indicators: 15-50 unique email prefixes sharing same P_emaildomain (catch-all) within 6 hours
Card1_txn_count_1day elevated 30-80x normal for domain, card1_amt_zscore >4.5
Initial test transactions $1-$15, then 1-4 transactions $200-$800 within 24 hours from same addr1
60-90% decline rate on test phase transactions, AVS mismatch on 40%+ attempts
Same device fingerprint or IP (dist1 <5km) across 10+ email prefix variations
ProductCD 'W' or 'C' exclusively, txn_hour clustering in 2-4 hour window during account creation
R_emaildomain often differs from P_emaildomain or null on successful high-value transactions
summary: A single fraudster creates 15-50 accounts using randomized email prefixes on the same catch-all domain (like protonmail.com or mail.ru) within hours, all from the same device or IP address. Small $1-$15 test transactions show 60-90% decline rates, then suddenly larger $200-$800 purchases appear from the same billing address using cards that passed the tests.
reasoning: The attacker is validating stolen credit cards at scale without triggering account-level blocks—each fake email prefix looks like a different customer, but they're all the same criminal testing which cards still work. Once a card authorizes on the small amount, they immediately exploit it for high-value fraud before the card owner notices.
indicator_explanations: Dozens of fake accounts registered using variations on one catch-all domain
Card sees wildly elevated transaction volume compared to normal domain behavior
Small tests followed quickly by large purchases from the same billing address
Most test transactions fail, many rejected for billing address verification mismatches
Same device or location cycles through multiple throwaway email accounts
Fraudster targets specific payment types during concentrated time windows for speed
Recipient email differs from purchaser email on successful high-dollar transactions
